elements.sent-2411-001: In recent years, \signSGD has garnered interest as both a practical optimizer as well as a simple model to understand adaptive optimizers like \Adam.
elements.sent-2411-002: Though there is a general consensus that \signSGD acts to precondition optimization and reshapes noise, quantitatively understanding these effects in theoretically solvable settings remains difficult.
elements.sent-2411-003: We present an analysis of \signSGD in a high dimensional limit, and derive a limiting SDE and ODE to describe the risk.
elements.sent-2411-004: Using this framework we quantify four effects of \signSGD: effective learning rate, noise compression, diagonal preconditioning, and gradient noise reshaping.
elements.sent-2411-005: Our analysis is consistent with experimental observations but moves beyond that by quantifying the dependence of these effects on the data and noise distributions.
elements.sent-2411-006: We conclude with a conjecture on how these results might be extended to \Adam.
elements.sent-2411-007: The success of deep learning has been driven by the effectiveness of relatively simple stochastic optimization algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-008: Stochastic gradient descent (\SGD) with momentum can be used to train models like ResNet50 with minimal hyperparameter tuning.
elements.sent-2411-009: The workhorse of modern machine learning is \Adam, which was designed to give an approximation of preconditioning with a diagonal, online approximation of the Fisher information matrix \citep{kingma2014adam .}
elements.sent-2411-010: Additional hypotheses for the success of \Adam include its ability to maintain balanced updates to parameters across layers and its potential noise-mitigating effects \citep{ zhang2020adaptivemethodsgoodattention,zhang2024transformers .}
elements.sent-2411-011: Getting a quantitative, theoretical understanding of Adam and its variants is hindered by their complexity.
elements.sent-2411-012: While the multiple exponential moving averages are easy to implement, they complicate analysis.
elements.sent-2411-013: The practical desire for simpler, more efficient learning algorithms as well as the theoretical desire for simpler models to analyze have led to a resurgence in the study of \signSGD.
elements.sent-2411-014: On average, \signSGD's updates at every step have positive dot product with the average \SGD step, but it can have dramatically different convergence properties \citep{signSGD_bernstein18a, karimireddy2019error .}
elements.sent-2411-015: Multiple studies point towards sign-based methods as an effective proxy given that the sign component of the gradient has been shown to play an important role in \Adam \citep{kunstner2023noise, balles_dissecting_adam, bernstein2018signsgd_vote .}
elements.sent-2411-016: Despite the promise of \signSGD, a detailed quantitative understanding of its dynamics in realistic settings remain elusive—in particular the nature of the preconditioning and the effect of the $\sgn$ function on the noise are not well understood.
elements.sent-2411-017: A crucial first step is to understand these effects on quadratic optimization problems.
elements.sent-2411-019: We derive a limiting stochastic differential equation (SDE) for \signSGD and combine it with a concentration result to derive a deterministic ordinary differential equation (ODE) that describes the dynamics of the risk in our setting (Section \ref{sec:signhsgd ).}
elements.sent-2411-020: We compare the dynamics of \signSGD and vanilla \SGD, isolating $4$ effects: \textcolor{lrcolor {effective learning rate , \textcolor{psicolor {noise-compression , \textcolor{dcolor {diagonal preconditioning , and \textcolor{Kcolor {gradient noise reshaping (Section \ref{sec:signsgd_comparison ).}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-021: We quantitatively analyze these four effects and their contributions to learning, including exact results in specific settings (remainder of Section \ref{sec:signsgd_comparison ).}
elements.sent-2411-022: Our work addresses significant technical challenges in analyzing both the preconditioning and noise transformation effects of \signSGD.
elements.sent-2411-023: Our analysis is consistent with more general experimental observations about adaptive methods, but provides a more quantitative understanding in our setting.
elements.sent-2411-024: We conclude with a discussion of the implications of our results for future study of adaptive algorithms, including a conjecture on the limiting form of \Adam in an equivalent setting.
elements.sent-2411-025: Our work considers linear regression using the mean-squared loss $\L$ in the one-pass scenario, where data is not reused.

elements.sent-2411-051: The analysis of \signSGD in high-dimensional settings presents a unique set of technical challenges and requires careful mathematical treatment.
elements.sent-2411-052: A core difficulty lies in the transformative effect of the sign operator on the gradient.
elements.sent-2411-053: Unlike traditional \SGD, where the gradient direction remains consistent with the magnitude of the update, \signSGD changes the gradient’s direction, via a non-Lipschitz compression operation.
elements.sent-2411-054: This compression not only changes the optimization landscape, but also impacts the interaction between the noise and the optimizer.
elements.sent-2411-055: Nonetheless, we show that under the assumptions above, there is a continuous stochastic process Sign-Homogenized SGD (\signHSGD) which captures the high-dimensional behaviour of \signSGD.

elements.sent-2411-427: In this section we derive a heuristic for \Adam \cite{kingma2014adam .}
elements.sent-2411-428: This is given, in our context, by: \begin{equation}Given: \quad \lr: learning rate \quad \beta_1, \beta_2 \in [0, 1): exponential decay rates for moment estimates \quad \epsilon_0: small constant for numerical stability Initialize: \quad \theta_0: initial parameter vector \quad m_0 \leftarrow 0: 1st moment vector \quad v_0 \leftarrow 0: 2nd moment vector \quad k \leftarrow 0: timestep Repeat until convergence: \quad k \leftarrow k + 1 \quad g_k \leftarrow \nabla_{\it} \L(\it_{k-1},\x_{k},\tar_{k}) \quad (Get gradients w.r.t. stochastic objective at timestep k) \quad m_k \leftarrow \beta_1 \cdot m_{k-1} + (1 - \beta_1) \cdot g_k \quad (Update biased first moment estimate) \quad v_k \leftarrow \beta_2 \cdot v_{k-1} + (1 - \beta_2) \cdot g_k^2 \quad (Update biased second raw moment estimate) \quad \hat{m}_k \leftarrow m_k / (1 - \beta_1^k) \quad (Compute bias-corrected first moment estimate) \quad \hat{v}_k \leftarrow v_k / (1 - \beta_2^k) \quad (Compute bias-corrected second raw moment estimate) \quad \theta_k \leftarrow \theta_{k-1} - \lr \hat{m}_k / (\sqrt{\hat{v}_k} + \epsilon_0) \quad (Update parameters)\end{equation} In a high-dimensional context, the first moment momentum $\beta_1$ has been observed to be equivalent to an effective change of learning rate, without inducing other benefits on the dynamics (see \cite{paquette2021dynamics ), and so we ignore it.}
elements.formula-2411-219: \begin{equation}Given: \quad \lr: learning rate \quad \beta_1, \beta_2 \in [0, 1): exponential decay rates for moment estimates \quad \epsilon_0: small constant for numerical stability Initialize: \quad \theta_0: initial parameter vector \quad m_0 \leftarrow 0: 1st moment vector \quad v_0 \leftarrow 0: 2nd moment vector \quad k \leftarrow 0: timestep Repeat until convergence: \quad k \leftarrow k + 1 \quad g_k \leftarrow \nabla_{\it} \L(\it_{k-1},\x_{k},\tar_{k}) \quad (Get gradients w.r.t. stochastic objective at timestep k) \quad m_k \leftarrow \beta_1 \cdot m_{k-1} + (1 - \beta_1) \cdot g_k \quad (Update biased first moment estimate) \quad v_k \leftarrow \beta_2 \cdot v_{k-1} + (1 - \beta_2) \cdot g_k^2 \quad (Update biased second raw moment estimate) \quad \hat{m}_k \leftarrow m_k / (1 - \beta_1^k) \quad (Compute bias-corrected first moment estimate) \quad \hat{v}_k \leftarrow v_k / (1 - \beta_2^k) \quad (Compute bias-corrected second raw moment estimate) \quad \theta_k \leftarrow \theta_{k-1} - \lr \hat{m}_k / (\sqrt{\hat{v}_k} + \epsilon_0) \quad (Update parameters)\end{equation}

elements.sent-2411-503: SignSGD provides a powerful tool for understanding and improving optimization algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-504: Notably, signSGD is particularly valuable for understanding the popular SGD variant, Adam.
elements.sent-2411-505: As revealed in \cite{balles_dissecting_adam , Adam's performance can be dissected into two key components: the direction, which is determined by the sign of the stochastic gradient, and the magnitude, which is influenced by the variance.}
elements.sent-2411-506: Remarkably, it was found that the sign component often dominates in determining the performance, suggesting that the full gradient might not be necessary.
elements.sent-2411-507: This insight highlights the usefulness of signSGD in providing a simplified yet powerful perspective on Adam's behavior.
elements.sent-2411-508: Beyond its conceptual utility, signSGD was introduced with the practical goal of improving efficiency by compressing the gradient.
elements.sent-2411-509: This is achieved by transmitting only the sign of each coordinate entry, resulting in a vector of $\{1, 0, -1\}$ \cite{signSGD_bernstein18a .}
elements.sent-2411-510: This technique not only reduces communication costs but can achieve or exceed SGD-levels of convergence \cite{signSGD_bernstein18a, raun_zeroth_oracle .}
elements.sent-2411-511: However, while these studies provide insights into long term convergence, they offer limited information on the dynamics of signSGD before convergence.
elements.sent-2411-512: In this project, we extend the analysis of signSGD by proposing a framework to study its dynamics in the high-dimensional limit through a related differential equation.
elements.sent-2411-514: A measure of relative variance for magnitude.
elements.sent-2411-515: Using this decomposition, \cite{balles_pedregosa2020_geom_signSGD argue that the sign relevant aspect of this decomposition is the driving force behind the effectiveness.}
elements.sent-2411-517: In this work, we consider linear regression using the mean-squared loss \begin{equation}\L (\it,\x,\tar) = \|\inner{\x}{\it} - \tar\|^2 / 2,\end{equation} in the streaming or one-pass scenario, where data is not reused.
elements.sent-2411-518: Sign SGD ($\sgn$-SGD), without mini-batching, is described by the iteration \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \label{eq:update} \it_{k+1} = \it_k - \lr_k \sgn \left (\nabla_{\it} \L(\it,\x,\tar) \right), \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $\sgn$ denotes the sign function applied element-wise and $\nabla_{\x \L(\it,\x,\tar) = (\inner{\x_{k+1 {\it_k - \tar_{k+1 )\x_{k+1}$ with initialization $\it_0 \in \R^d$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-519: We assume that the samples $\{(\x_k,\tar_k)\}_{k\geq 0}$, consisting of data $\x_k$ and targets $\tar_k$, satisfy the following:.
elements.section-2411-013: A heuristic calculation for more general distributions
elements.sent-2411-520: We will need to compute \begin{equation} \E[\sgn(\ell_{\it}) \sgn(\x)] = \E[\sgn(\inner{\x}{\v_k} - \eps) \sgn(\x)] \end{equation} Consider, conditional on $\x_i$, where $a_i = \sum_{j\neq i \v_i \x_i$ \begin{equation} \E[\sgn(\inner{\x}{\v_k} - \eps) | \x_i] = \int \sgn(a_i - \eps + \v_i \x_i) d \mu_{\ell_{|\x_i}} = \prob(a_i - \eps > -\v_i \x_i | \x_i) - \prob(a_i - \eps < -\v_i \x_i | \x_i) \end{equation} Let's say the data is Gaussian.}

elements.sent-2411-524: Finally, \begin{equation} b^2 = 2 \Ri_i - \rho_i ^2 \K_{ii} = (2 \Ri - 2\sum_{j=1}^d \K_{ij}\v_i \v_j + \K_{ii} v_i^2) - \left(\inner{\Kbar_i}{\v}^2 - 2 \sum_{j=1}^d \K_{ij} \v_i \v_j + \K_{ii}\v_i^2 \right) = 2 \Ri - \inner{\Kbar_i}{\v}^2 \end{equation} Thus, \begin{equation} \prob(a_i - \eps > -\v_i \x_i | \x_i) = \prob(\rho_i \x_i + b z_2 - \eps > -\v_i \x_i | \x_i) = \prob(b z_2 - \eps > -(\v_i + \rho_i )\x_i | \x_i) = \int_{-(\v_i + \rho_i)\x_i}^\infty h(x) dx \end{equation} where \begin{equation}h(x) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2 \pi b^2}} \int_{-\infty}^\infty \exp\left(\frac{-(x+y)^2}{2b^2} \right) \,d \L_\epsilon(y).\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-556: First consider vanilla SGD, whose long term behaviour can be found by solving \begin{equation}0 = -2 \alpha \lr R_t + \lr^2 \alpha^2 (R_t + \sigma^2 /2)\end{equation} This implies \begin{equation}R_\infty = \frac{\lr \alpha \sigma^2}{2(2 - \alpha \lr)}\end{equation} for any $\lr < 2 / \alpha$.
elements.sent-2411-557: Meanwhile, for signSGD, we solve \begin{equation}0 = \frac{- 4 \sqrt{\alpha} \lr_t \Rsgn_t}{\pi \sqrt{2\Rsgn_t+\eta^2}} + \frac{\lr_t^2 \alpha}{2}\end{equation} so \begin{equation}64 R^2 - 2 \lr^2 \pi^2 \alpha - \lr^2 \alpha \pi^2 \sigma^2 = 0\end{equation} (note that this means $R_\infty > 0$ if $\sigma > 0 $).
elements.sent-2411-558: So overall, \begin{equation}R_\infty = \frac{\lr^2 \pi^2 \alpha + \lr \pi \sqrt{\lr^2 \pi^2 \alpha^2 + 64 \alpha \sigma^2}}{64}\end{equation} in particular $\sigma = 0$ implies \begin{equation}R_\infty = \lr^2 \pi^2 \alpha / 32\end{equation} let $\lr = \eta / \alpha$ for a fixed $0< \eta < 2$, then SGD gives \begin{equation}R_\infty = \frac{\eta \sigma^2}{2 ( 2- \eta)}\end{equation} while sign gives \begin{equation}R_\infty = \frac{\eta^2 \pi^2 / \alpha + \eta / \alpha \sqrt{\eta^2 \pi^2 + 64 \alpha \sigma^2}}{64}\end{equation} idk, is that interesting? For $\eps \sim \Exp - 1$ and data $\alpha$-isotropic, we have \begin{equation} h_0(t) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{4 R_t \pi}} \int_{-1}^\infty e^{\frac{-x^2}{4R}} e^{-(x+1)}dx = \frac{1}{\sqrt{4 R_t \pi}} \sqrt{R_t \pi} \erfc\left ( \frac{2R_t-1}{\sqrt{4R_t}}\right)e^{R_t -1} = \frac{1}{2}\erfc\left ( \frac{2R_t-1}{\sqrt{4R_t}}\right)e^{R_t -1} \end{equation} So, our quantity of interest becomes \begin{equation}\frac{2}{\pi} (2R_t + 1) \erfc^2\left ( \frac{2R_t-1}{\sqrt{4R_t}}\right)e^{2R_t -2}\end{equation} Plotting things, for small $R$ this value is less than $1$ and appears monotone decreasing.
elements.sent-2411-566: Also, using a bound I found online, and letting $R_t$ be large \begin{equation} \frac{2}{\pi} (2R_t + 1) \erfc^2\left ( \sqrt{R}\right)e^{2R_t -2} \leq \frac{8}{\pi^2} (2R_t + 1) e^{2R_t -2} \frac{e^{-2R_t}}{(\sqrt{R_t} + \sqrt{R_t + 4/\pi})^2} = \frac{8}{\pi^2} (2R_t + 1) e^{-2} \frac{1}{(\sqrt{R_t} + \sqrt{R_t + 4/\pi})^2} \to \frac{16}{\pi^2} e^{-2} \approx 0.22 \end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-913: This document provides a basic paper template and submission guidelines.
elements.sent-2411-914: Abstracts must be a single paragraph, ideally between 4--6 sentences long.
elements.sent-2411-915: Gross violations will trigger corrections at the camera-ready phase.
elements.sent-2411-916: Submission to ICML 2025 will be entirely electronic, via a web site (not email).
elements.sent-2411-917: Information about the submission process and \LaTeX\ templates are available on the conference web site at: \begin{center http://icml.cc/ \end{center The guidelines below will be enforced for initial submissions and camera-ready copies.}}
elements.sent-2411-920: If your paper has appendices, submit the appendix together with the main body and the references as a single file.
elements.sent-2411-921: Reviewers will not look for appendices as a separate PDF file.
elements.sent-2411-922: So if you submit such an extra file, reviewers will very likely miss it.
elements.sent-2411-923: Page limit: The main body of the paper has to be fitted to 8 pages, excluding references and appendices; the space for the latter two is not limited in pages, but the total file size may not exceed 10MB.
elements.sent-2411-924: For the final version of the paper, authors can add one extra page to the main body.
elements.sent-2411-925: Do not include author information or acknowledgements in your initial submission.
elements.sent-2411-926: Your paper should be in 10 point Times font.
elements.sent-2411-927: Make sure your PDF file only uses Type-1 fonts.
elements.sent-2411-928: Place figure captions under the figure (and omit titles from inside the graphic file itself).
elements.sent-2411-929: Place table captions over the table.
elements.sent-2411-930: References must include page numbers whenever possible and be as complete as possible.
elements.sent-2411-931: Place multiple citations in chronological order.
elements.sent-2411-932: Do not alter the style template; in particular, do not compress the paper format by reducing the vertical spaces.
elements.sent-2411-933: Keep your abstract brief and self-contained, one paragraph and roughly 4--6 sentences.
elements.sent-2411-934: Gross violations will require correction at the camera-ready phase.
elements.sent-2411-935: The title should have content words capitalized.
elements.sent-2411-936: Anonymous Submission: ICML uses double-blind review: no identifying author information may appear on the title page or in the paper itself.
elements.sent-2411-937: Authors must provide their manuscripts in PDF format.
elements.sent-2411-938: Furthermore, please make sure that files contain only embedded Type-1 fonts (e.g.,~using the program pdffonts in linux or using File/DocumentProperties/Fonts in Acrobat).
elements.sent-2411-939: Other fonts (like Type-3) might come from graphics files imported into the document.
elements.sent-2411-940: Authors using Word must convert their document to PDF\@.

elements.sent-2411-942: Submissions will not be accepted in Word format or any format other than PDF\@.. Really.

elements.sent-2411-944: Those who use \LaTeX should avoid including Type-3 fonts.

elements.sent-2411-946: It is a zero following the ``-G'', which tells dvips to use the config.pdf file.
elements.sent-2411-947: Newer \TeX\ distributions don't always need this option.
elements.sent-2411-948: Using pdflatex rather than latex, often gives better results.
elements.sent-2411-949: This program avoids the Type-3 font problem, and supports more advanced features in the microtype package.
elements.sent-2411-950: Graphics files should be a reasonable size, and included from an appropriate format.
elements.sent-2411-951: Use vector formats (.eps/.pdf) for plots, lossless bitmap formats (.png) for raster graphics with sharp lines, and jpeg for photo-like images.
elements.sent-2411-952: The style file uses the hyperref package to make clickable links in documents.

elements.sent-2411-960: Camera-ready copies should have the title of the paper as running head on each page except the first one.
elements.sent-2411-961: The running title consists of a single line centered above a horizontal rule which is $1$~point thick.
elements.sent-2411-962: The running head should be centered, bold and in $9$~point type.
elements.sent-2411-963: The rule should be $10$~points above the main text.
elements.sent-2411-964: For those using the \LaTeX style file, the original title is automatically set as running head using the fancyhdr package which is included in the ICML 2025 style file package.

elements.sent-2411-966: Authors using Word must edit the header of the document themselves.
elements.sent-2411-967: All submissions must follow the specified format.
elements.sent-2411-968: The text of the paper should be formatted in two columns, with an overall width of 6.75~inches, height of 9.0~inches, and 0.25~inches between the columns.
elements.sent-2411-969: The left margin should be 0.75~inches and the top margin 1.0~inch (2.54~cm).
elements.sent-2411-970: The right and bottom margins will depend on whether you print on US letter or A4 paper, but all final versions must be produced for US letter size.
elements.sent-2411-971: Do not write anything on the margins.
elements.sent-2411-972: The paper body should be set in 10~point type with a vertical spacing of 11~points.
elements.sent-2411-973: Please use Times typeface throughout the text.
elements.sent-2411-974: The paper title should be set in 14~point bold type and centered between two horizontal rules that are 1~point thick, with 1.0~inch between the top rule and the top edge of the page.
elements.sent-2411-975: Capitalize the first letter of content words and put the rest of the title in lower case.
elements.sent-2411-976: ICML uses double-blind review, so author information must not appear.
elements.sent-2411-977: If you are using \LaTeX\/ and the icml2025.sty file, use \verb+\icmlauthor{...}
elements.sent-2411-978: Submissions that include the author information will not be reviewed.

elements.sent-2411-980: In particular, do not use phrases that reveal your identity (e.g., ``in previous work \cite{langley00 , we have shown \ldots'').}
elements.sent-2411-981: Do not anonymize citations in the reference section.

elements.sent-2411-986: If a paper is accepted, a final camera-ready copy must be prepared.
elements.sent-2411-987: For camera-ready papers, author information should start 0.3~inches below the bottom rule surrounding the title.
elements.sent-2411-988: The authors' names should appear in 10~point bold type, in a row, separated by white space, and centered.
elements.sent-2411-989: Author names should not be broken across lines.
elements.sent-2411-990: Unbolded superscripted numbers, starting 1, should be used to refer to affiliations.

elements.sent-2411-1001: The paper abstract should begin in the left column, 0.4~inches below the final address.
elements.sent-2411-1002: The heading `Abstract' should be centered, bold, and in 11~point type.
elements.sent-2411-1003: The abstract body should use 10~point type, with a vertical spacing of 11~points, and should be indented 0.25~inches more than normal on left-hand and right-hand margins.
elements.sent-2411-1004: Insert 0.4~inches of blank space after the body.
elements.sent-2411-1005: Keep your abstract brief and self-contained, limiting it to one paragraph and roughly 4--6 sentences.
elements.sent-2411-1006: You should organize your paper into sections and paragraphs to help readers place a structure on the material and understand its contributions.

elements.sent-2411-1008: Leave 0.25~inches of space before the heading and 0.15~inches after the heading.

elements.sent-2411-1010: Leave 0.2~inches of space before the heading and 0.13~inches afterward.

elements.sent-2411-1013: Please use no more than three levels of headings.
elements.sent-2411-1014: Within each section or subsection, you should further partition the paper into paragraphs.

For the CIFAR10 dataset, we validate the results of Theorem \ref{thm:odeconvergence} which gives the limit risk of \signODE under Gaussian data.
